turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in case 22-381, Yegiazaryan v. Smagin.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Petitioner is a former Russian businessman who defrauded respondent, another Russian businessman. To avoid a criminal indictment in Russia for that fraud, petitioner fled to a mansion to Beverly Hills, where he has lived ever since. Meanwhile, respondent won an arbitration award against petitioner for the amount he was defrauded over $80 million. Seeking to collect on that award, respondent filed an enforcement action in a California District Court. The District Court issued an order freezing petitioner's California assets and entered a California judgment against petitioner in the amount of that award. To avoid the asset freeze, petitioner is alleged to have created a complex web of offshore entities to hide his money, which included a $198 million settlement obtained in another matter. Petitioner also engaged in various other alleged schemes to defeat respondent's collection efforts including for example, submitting a false doctor's note to the California District Court and then intimidating the doctor involved. All of these alleged actions either took place in the United States or were directed by petitioner from his Beverly Hills mansion with the aim of defeating collection of the California judgment. Respondent frustrated with his inability to collect on the judgment sued petitioner in 2020 in California under the RICO statute, which stands for the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act, an act designed to address the unlawful activities of those engaged in organized crime; specifically, respondent's private civil RICO suit alleged that petitioner had prevented collection on the California judgment through a pattern of criminal activity including wire fraud, witness tampering and obstruction of justice. The District Court dismissed the RICO complaint on the ground that respondent had failed to plead a domestic injury as required by this Court's decision in RJR Nabisco, Inc. v. European Community. The District Court assigned great weight to the fact that respondent lives in Russia. The Ninth Circuit reversed adopting a more contextual approach focused on the nature of the alleged injury and the fact that many actions giving rise to that injury occurred in California. The Ninth Circuit was correct in adopting a contextual approach that looks to the circumstances surrounding the injury when assessing where it arose. Respondent has alleged that his efforts to execute on a California judgment in California against a California resident were undermined by actions that largely occurred in California over directed from and targeted at California. In the context of this case, these facts suffice to state a domestic injury under RICO. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Ninth Circuit. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justice Thomas has joined and which Justice Gorsuch has joined as to part one.
